title: OOP: Inheritance, Polymorphism & Interfaces
description: Master Object-Oriented Programming in Java: inheritance, method overriding, polymorphism, abstract classes, and interfaces.
learning_outcomes: Override methods and use the super keyword correctly
Understand and apply polymorphism in real-world scenarios
Design programs using abstract classes and interfaces
Use instanceof and type casting safely
Apply the protected access modifier appropriately
Implement multiple interfaces in a single class
Understand the difference between abstract classes and interfaces
Use the final keyword to prevent inheritance and overriding
Override toString(), equals(), and hashCode() from the Object class

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Explains OOP like family trees and job roles.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey, still there? OOP has a lot of new words -- inheritance, polymorphism, abstract... If any of these feel confusing, just ask me! I can explain them with everyday examples like family traits or job descriptions.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Taking a moment to think is great -- OOP concepts click at different speeds for everyone. Would it help if I showed you a quick picture of how classes connect to each other? Sometimes seeing the family tree makes it all make sense.
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush at all! OOP is one of those topics where a short break actually helps your brain connect the dots. Take 5 minutes, grab a drink, and when you come back I will give you a fresh recap of exactly where you left off.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Nice try! Errors in OOP are super common -- even experienced developers mix up extends vs implements, or forget to call super(). Let me show you exactly what happened and why. You are learning, and that is what matters.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see this one is tricky. Let me break it down differently. Think of it this way: a parent class is like a recipe template. The child class copies the whole recipe but can change specific ingredients. Want me to walk through it line by line?
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just used one of the most powerful ideas in programming. Every big application -- from games to banking apps -- uses exactly what you just wrote. On to the next one!

encouragement_phrases: OOP is like learning a new language -- it clicks suddenly after enough practice!
You are building the same skills that professional Java developers use every day.
Every class you write is a building block. You are getting stronger!
Inheritance might feel abstract now, but it is everywhere in real code.
Polymorphism is just a fancy word for one name, many forms. You have got this!
Interfaces are like contracts -- and you are learning to write them like a pro.
That was a tough one and you nailed it! Take a moment to feel good about that.
Mistakes are not failures -- they are your brain making new connections.
You are further along than you think. Look how much you have already done!
The fact that you are here working through this shows real dedication.

explanation_styles.analogy: Inheritance is like a family tree. A child inherits traits from parents but can develop unique ones. In Java, a child class inherits methods and fields from a parent class but can override them.
explanation_styles.visual: Picture a pyramid: Object at top. Below it, Animal. Below Animal, Dog and Cat side by side. Each level inherits everything above and adds its own features.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Write parent class. Step 2: Write class Child extends Parent. Step 3: Child gets all parent methods. Step 4: Override with @Override. Step 5: Test.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Dog d = new Dog(); d.speak(); -- Java looks in Dog first. If found, runs Dog version. If not, goes up to Animal. This is the lookup chain.
explanation_styles.comparison: Abstract class = partially-built house (some rooms done). Interface = blueprint (just the plan). A class can extend ONE abstract class but implement MANY interfaces.

theory.introduction:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is the backbone of Java. In the previous topic, you learned how to create classes and objects -- think of those as blueprints and buildings. Now we go to the next level: what happens when buildings share features?

Imagine a family. Parents pass traits to their children -- eye colour, height, talents. But children are not exact clones. They inherit the basics and develop their own unique abilities. That is exactly what INHERITANCE does in Java.

Now imagine a remote control. You press the play button, but what happens depends on the device -- a TV plays a show, a music player plays a song. Same button, different behaviour. That is POLYMORPHISM.

Finally, imagine job descriptions. A Driver must be able to start, stop, and steer. Whether they drive a car, bus, or truck, the same description applies. That is what INTERFACES do -- they define what a class MUST be able to do, without saying HOW.

These three ideas -- inheritance, polymorphism, and interfaces -- are the foundation of professional Java programming. Every Android app, every web server, every game uses them.
theory.why_important: OOP is not just theory -- it is how real software is built. Without inheritance, you would copy-paste code into every class. Without polymorphism, you would need separate methods for every type. Without interfaces, teams could not agree on how code connects. Learning OOP means you can read and write professional Java code, contribute to real projects, and pass job interviews.

key_concepts.concept: Inheritance -- The Family Tree of Classes
key_concepts.explanation:
Inheritance lets one class (child) receive all fields and methods of another (parent). Use the keyword extends.

Think of a family recipe book. Grandmother wrote a tomato sauce recipe. Mother inherited it but added basil -- her own twist. She did not rewrite the whole recipe, she extended it.

Why use it? Without inheritance, if you had 10 types of Vehicle, you would copy start(), stop(), getFuel() into each. With inheritance, write once in Vehicle, every child gets them free.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of extends like saying 'I am a type of'. Dog extends Animal means a Dog IS a type of Animal.

key_concepts.concept: The super Keyword -- Calling Your Parent
key_concepts.explanation:
super has two uses:
1. super() calls the parent constructor. Must be FIRST line in child constructor.
2. super.method() calls the parent version of a method.

Think of building a house: parent builds foundation (super()), child adds interior design on top.
key_concepts.ai_hint: If you forget super() and the parent has no zero-argument constructor, Java gives a compile error.

key_concepts.concept: Method Overriding -- Same Name, New Behaviour
key_concepts.explanation:
When a child writes its own version of a parent method, that is overriding. The child version replaces the parent version.

Like a restaurant chain: headquarters has a standard burger. Each branch can override it -- Mexico adds jalapenos, Japan adds teriyaki.

Rules: same name, same parameters, use @Override annotation.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Always write @Override above your method. If you typo the name, @Override catches it at compile time.

key_concepts.explanation:
Polymorphism means a parent-type variable can hold a child-type object, and Java runs the child version of methods.

Like a universal remote: press play and the device decides what happens. Same button, different behaviour.

Java decides which method version to run at RUNTIME (dynamic dispatch).
key_concepts.ai_hint: Variable type determines WHAT you can call. Object type determines HOW it behaves.

key_concepts.explanation:
An abstract class is intentionally incomplete. It says: some features are ready, some MUST be filled in by children.

Like a job template: company name and benefits are filled in, but Job Title and Salary are left blank for each department.

Key rules: cannot create objects from it, abstract methods have no body, children MUST implement all abstract methods.
key_concepts.ai_hint: If you see 'cannot instantiate abstract class', you tried to create an object from it directly.

key_concepts.explanation:
An interface is a contract: any class that signs it MUST provide certain methods.

Like a UK power socket: the socket does not care if a lamp or TV is plugged in, as long as it has the right 3 pins.

Key rules: use 'implements' not extends, all methods public by default, a class can implement MULTIPLE interfaces.
key_concepts.ai_hint: extends for classes (ONE parent), implements for interfaces (MANY). A class can do both.

key_concepts.concept: Abstract Class vs Interface -- When to Use Which
key_concepts.explanation:
Use abstract class when: sharing CODE between related classes, natural is-a relationship, need constructors.

Use interface when: defining a CAPABILITY for unrelated classes, need multiple contracts, maximum flexibility.

Abstract class = What something IS (Animal, Vehicle). Interface = What something CAN DO (Drawable, Comparable).
key_concepts.ai_hint: If unsure, start with interface. You can always add abstract class later.

key_concepts.explanation:
instanceof checks the actual type of an object. Returns true if the object IS that type or a subtype.

Like checking a package label before opening it: 'Is this fragile? Is this food?'

Always check instanceof BEFORE downcasting to avoid ClassCastException.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Always check instanceof BEFORE casting. No check = risk of ClassCastException.

key_concepts.explanation:
Upcasting: child to parent type. AUTOMATIC, always safe. Like calling a Labrador simply a dog.

Downcasting: parent to child type. MANUAL, can be dangerous. Like assuming a dog is a Labrador -- it might be a Poodle.

Upcast: lose child-specific methods. Downcast: regain them, but risk ClassCastException.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Upcasting = always safe, automatic. Downcasting = check instanceof first, cast explicitly.

key_concepts.explanation:
protected means: children and same package can see it, but nobody else.

Like a family recipe shared with family members but not the public.

Access levels from most open to most restricted: public > protected > (default) > private.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Use protected when children need access but outsiders should not.

key_concepts.concept: The final Keyword -- No More Changes
key_concepts.explanation:
final has three uses:
1. final variable = constant, cannot change
2. final method = cannot be overridden
3. final class = cannot have children

String is a final class in Java -- nobody can extend it. This is a security decision.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Use final for things that should NEVER change: constants, security-critical methods, classes.

key_concepts.concept: Object Class -- The Ancestor of Everything
key_concepts.explanation:
Every class extends Object automatically. Object provides toString(), equals(), hashCode().

Override toString() for readable output. Override equals() to compare values instead of memory addresses. If you override equals, also override hashCode.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Always override toString() -- it makes debugging much easier.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Simple Inheritance -- Animal and Dog
complete_examples.description: Shows the most basic inheritance: child extends parent and adds its own method.
complete_examples.output:
Buddy is eating.
Buddy is sleeping.
Buddy says: Woof!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Dog extends Animal so it has EVERYTHING Animal has plus its own extras. name and eat() come from Animal. bark() is Dog-only.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a Cat class with a purr() method. Create a Cat and call eat(), sleep(), and purr().

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Constructor Chaining with super()
complete_examples.description: Shows how constructors chain from child to parent.
complete_examples.output:
Person constructor
Student constructor
GradStudent constructor
Alice - AI Safety
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Constructors run from TOP of hierarchy DOWN. Person first, then Student, then GradStudent. Like building: foundation first, then walls, then roof.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Remove super() from Student. Read the error. Then fix it.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Method Overriding with @Override
complete_examples.description: Shows how child classes provide their own implementation of parent methods.
complete_examples.output:
Guitar strums: twang twang!
Drum beats: boom boom!
Tuning Guitar
Tuning Drum
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Guitar and Drum both override play() but neither overrides tune(). tune() uses the parent version. @Override is optional but catches typos.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a Piano class with its own play(). Also override tune().

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Polymorphism with Array
complete_examples.description: The real power: process different objects through one loop.
complete_examples.output:
Circle: area = 78.54
Rectangle: area = 24.00
Triangle: area = 12.00
Total: 114.54
complete_examples.ai_explanation: shapes[] holds Shape references but actual objects are Circle, Rect, Triangle. The loop calls the RIGHT version of area() for each. Add a Pentagon tomorrow and the loop works unchanged.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a Square class (extends Rect, one side). Add it to the array.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Abstract Employee Payroll
complete_examples.description: Abstract classes enforce a contract while sharing common code.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Employee is abstract -- cannot instantiate it. calculatePay() is abstract -- each type has its own formula. printPayslip() is concrete and shared. Bob: 40*15 + 5*15*1.5 = 600 + 112.50 = 712.50.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add ContractEmployee with a fixed project fee divided by months.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Multiple Interfaces
complete_examples.description: A class can implement multiple interfaces for different abilities.
complete_examples.output:
Printing: Report
Saving Report to report.pdf
Sharing Report with boss@co.com
Printing: Report
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Document wears three hats. Through a Printable reference, only print() is visible. A class can implement as many interfaces as needed.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create Photo implementing Printable and Shareable but NOT Saveable.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Abstract + Interface Combined
complete_examples.description: Real-world pattern: extend abstract class AND implement interface.
complete_examples.output:
DL-4521 drove 50km. Fuel: 90%
DL-4521 now at: Customer
DL-4521 refuelled
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Vehicle = what it IS. Trackable = what it CAN DO. DeliveryTruck combines both. A PersonalCar could extend Vehicle without Trackable.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create PersonalCar (Vehicle but NOT Trackable). Create PhoneApp (Trackable but NOT Vehicle).

complete_examples.title: Example 8: instanceof and Safe Downcasting
complete_examples.description: Check object types and cast safely.
complete_examples.output:
Rex: Woof!
Rex fetches!
Luna: Meow!
Luna purrs...
Max: Woof!
Max fetches!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Check instanceof BEFORE casting. Without it, casting Cat to Dog crashes with ClassCastException.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Remove the instanceof check. Run it. See ClassCastException. Then put it back.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: protected Modifier
complete_examples.description: protected = children and same package can see it.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: balance is protected: SavingsAccount can read it. deposit() is protected: SavingsAccount can call it. number is private: SavingsAccount cannot see it.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try accessing number from SavingsAccount. What error?

complete_examples.title: Example 10: final Keyword
complete_examples.description: final variable = constant. final method = cannot override. final class = cannot extend.
complete_examples.output:
Playing chess
Max: 4. No cheating!
Pi = 3.14159265
complete_examples.ai_explanation: MathConst is final class: nobody can extend it. showRules is final method: Chess cannot override it. MAX_PLAYERS is final variable: cannot change.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try changing MAX_PLAYERS inside Chess. Try extending MathConst. Read the errors.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: toString and equals Override
complete_examples.description: Override Object methods for human-readable output and value comparison.
complete_examples.output:
Book{1984 by Orwell}
true
false
false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Without toString: prints Book@4a5b6c. With it: prints Book{1984 by Orwell}. equals compares values. == compares memory addresses (always false for different objects).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add hashCode() using Objects.hash(title, author). Rule: override equals -> must override hashCode.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Default Methods (Java 8+)
complete_examples.description: Interfaces can have default methods with actual code since Java 8.
complete_examples.output:
[CON] Start
[CON] ERROR: Disk
[CON] WARNING: Low mem
[FILE] Start
[FILE] !!! Disk !!!
[FILE] WARNING: Low mem
complete_examples.ai_explanation: ConsoleLogger gets logError and logWarn for free from the interface. FileLogger overrides logError but uses default logWarn.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a static factory method: static Logger console() { return new ConsoleLogger(); }

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Multi-Level Inheritance
complete_examples.description: Three-level hierarchy showing how methods cascade.
complete_examples.output:
Canine breathes through lungs
Rex runs on 4 legs
Rex: Woof!
Alive: true
true true true
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Chain: Dog -> Animal -> LivingThing -> Object. breathe() found in Animal (overrides LivingThing). move() found in Dog. alive found in LivingThing.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add Fish extends Animal. Override breathe (gills). Override move (swims).

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Strategy Pattern with Interfaces
complete_examples.description: Interfaces enable swappable behaviour -- the Strategy design pattern.
complete_examples.output:
Using: Bubble Sort
1 2 5 8 9 
Using: Selection Sort
1 2 5 8 9
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Processor does not know or care which sort is used -- it just calls strategy.sort(). You can add InsertionSort tomorrow without changing Processor. This is how real apps work.

visual_diagram.title: OOP Concepts Map -- Inheritance, Polymorphism & Interfaces
visual_diagram.content: OOP CONCEPTS MAP [START] You have a class | v Do other classes share its features? / \ YES NO | | v v Use INHERITANCE Keep standalone | v class Child extends Parent { } | v Should parent be creatable? / \ YES NO | | v v Normal class Make it ABSTRACT [METHOD OVERRIDING] Parent: void speak() { "generic" } | v Child needs different behaviour? / \ YES NO | | v v @Override Inherit as-is [POLYMORPHISM] Animal pet = new Dog(); Variable type: Animal (what you CAN call) Object type: Dog (HOW it behaves) pet.speak() ---> Dog.speak() runs! pet.fetch() ---> COMPILE ERROR [ABSTRACT vs INTERFACE] ABSTRACT CLASS INTERFACE What something IS What something CAN DO Has constructors No constructors Can have fields Only constants Extend ONE only Implement MANY Dog IS an Animal Dog CAN Swim [CASTING] UPCAST (safe, auto): Dog --> Animal DOWNCAST (risky): Animal --> Dog (check instanceof first!) [REMEMBER] 1. extends = ONE parent 2. implements = MANY interfaces 3. @Override = new behaviour 4. Polymorphism = parent var, child obj 5. abstract = cannot instantiate 6. interface = contract/capabilities 7. final = locked forever 8. Always instanceof before downcast

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Your First Child Class
practice_exercises.description: Create a Dog class that extends Animal. Add bark().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's start simple! Make a class that inherits from another.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Buddy is eating.
Buddy says Woof!

hints.message: Start with: class Dog extends Animal { }
Add: void bark() { System.out.println(name + " says Woof!"); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Predict the Output -- Inheritance
practice_exercises.description: Read code, predict output without running.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Read each line slowly. The child inherits EVERYTHING from parent.

hints.message: c.type = 'Sedan' changes the inherited field.
start() prints the type field which is now 'Sedan'.

Output:
Sedan starting
Beep beep!
Doors: 4

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Fix the Bug -- Missing extends
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix why Microwave cannot use brand.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Something is missing in the class declaration...

hints.message: Microwave needs to be a type of Appliance.
Change: class Microwave extends Appliance
Fix: class Microwave extends Appliance { ... }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Build a Vehicle Hierarchy
practice_exercises.description: Create Vehicle parent, Car and Motorcycle children.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: What ALL vehicles share goes in Vehicle. Unique stuff in children.

hints.message: class Vehicle { String brand; int year; void describe() { System.out.println(brand + " (" + year + ")"); } }
class Car extends Vehicle { int doors; @Override void describe() { super.describe(); System.out.println("Doors: " + doors); } }
class Motorcycle extends Vehicle { boolean hasSidecar; @Override void describe() { super.describe(); System.out.println("Sidecar: " + hasSidecar); } }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Constructor Chaining
practice_exercises.description: Fix Student to call Person's constructor with super().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: super() must be the FIRST line in child constructor.

hints.message: Add super(name, age); as the VERY FIRST line.
Student(String name, int age, String id) { super(name, age); this.id = id; }
super() MUST be first. It sets up the parent part before child adds extras.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Access Levels Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Determine which lines compile and which give errors.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: private = only the class itself. protected = children too. public = everyone.

hints.message: public and protected are visible to children. private is NOT.
pub=COMPILES, prot=COMPILES, priv=ERROR, pubMethod=COMPILES, privMethod=ERROR
Remove the private lines to make code compile. Private means only Parent itself can see it.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Three-Level Hierarchy
practice_exercises.description: Create LivingThing -> Animal -> Cat, three levels deep.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Like a family tree with three generations.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Alive: true
Breathing...
Luna is eating
Luna is purring

hints.message: class LivingThing { boolean alive = true; void breathe() { System.out.println("Breathing..."); } }
class Animal extends LivingThing { String name; void eat() { System.out.println(name + " is eating"); } }
class Cat extends Animal { void purr() { System.out.println(name + " is purring"); } }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Fix -- super() Order
practice_exercises.description: super() must be the first line. Fix the order.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Java has a strict rule: super() comes before anything else.

hints.message: Swap the two lines in the constructor.
Put super(m, p); first, then this.legs = legs;
Chair(String m, double p, int legs) { super(m, p); this.legs = legs; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Using super.method()
practice_exercises.description: Override print() but also call parent's version.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: super.method() gives you the parent's original version.

hints.message: @Override void print(String text) { super.print(text); ... }
Add System.out.println("Colour: " + colour); after super.print(text);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: School System
practice_exercises.description: Person -> Teacher (subject), Student (grade). Each has introduce().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: What ALL people share (name, age) vs unique to each role.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Hi, I'm Mr Smith, age 45, I teach Maths
Hi, I'm Emma, age 16, grade 11

hints.message: Person: void introduce() { System.out.print("Hi, I'm "+name+", age "+age); }
Teacher: @Override void introduce() { super.introduce(); System.out.println(", I teach "+subject); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Override toString()
practice_exercises.description: Make Product print nicely with toString().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: toString() is the most commonly overridden method.

hints.message: @Override public String toString() { return ...; }
return "Product{name='" + name + "', price=" + price + "}";
@Override public String toString() { return "Product{name='" + name + "', price=" + price + "}"; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Override equals()
practice_exercises.description: Make Point compare values, not memory addresses.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Default equals checks same object. Override to check same VALUES.

hints.message: Pattern: if(this==o) true; if(!(o instanceof Point)) false; cast; compare fields.
@Override public boolean equals(Object o) { if(this==o) return true; if(!(o instanceof Point)) return false; Point p=(Point)o; return x==p.x && y==p.y; }
Memorise: check ref, check type, cast, compare. This is the standard Java equals pattern.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Predict -- Which Override Runs?
practice_exercises.description: Trace which method version executes.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: If child overrides, child version runs. If not, parent's.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Hello from B
Bye from A

hints.message: B overrides greet() but NOT farewell().
greet: B's version. farewell: A's version (not overridden).

Hello from B
Bye from A

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Fix -- Wrong Override Signature
practice_exercises.description: Override has wrong parameter types -- it is overLOADING not overRIDING.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Parameters MUST match exactly for overriding.

hints.message: Parent uses (double,double). Child uses (int,int). Not the same!
Change to: double add(double a, double b)
@Override double add(double a, double b) { System.out.println("Smart!"); return a+b+0.5; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Override + super Call
practice_exercises.description: Override log() to add timestamp, then call parent's version.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: super.method() runs the original, then you add your twist.

hints.message: @Override void log(String msg) { System.out.print("[TIME] "); super.log(msg); }
System.out.print prints without newline, then super.log adds the rest.
@Override void log(String msg) { System.out.print("[TIME] "); super.log(msg); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Shape Area Overrides
practice_exercises.description: Circle and Square each calculate area differently.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each shape overrides area() with its own formula.

hints.message: class Circle extends Shape { double r; Circle(double r){this.r=r;} @Override double area(){return Math.PI*r*r;} }
class Square extends Shape { double s; Square(double s){this.s=s;} @Override double area(){return s*s;} }
Each child overrides area() with its formula. The parent's area()=0 is the default.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: @Override Safety Net
practice_exercises.description: See how @Override catches typos.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: @Override tells Java 'I intend to override'. Typos get caught.

hints.message: Java is case-sensitive: 'makesound' is NOT 'makeSound'.
Change to: void makeSound() -- capital S.
Without @Override, Java silently creates a new method. @Override catches the typo at compile time.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Product describe() Override
practice_exercises.description: Different products describe themselves differently.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each product type adds its own details to the base description.

hints.message: Book: @Override String describe() { return super.describe() + " by " + author; }
Electronics: @Override String describe() { return super.describe() + " (" + warrantyMonths + "mo warranty)"; }

class Book extends Product { String author; Book(String n,double p,String a){super(n,p);author=a;} @Override String describe(){return super.describe()+" by "+author;} }
class Electronics extends Product { int wm; Electronics(String n,double p,int w){super(n,p);wm=w;} @Override String describe(){return super.describe()+" ("+wm+"mo warranty)";} }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Polymorphic Variable
practice_exercises.description: One variable, many forms -- the core of polymorphism.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same variable, same method call, different output!

hints.message: Animal pet = new Dog(); is valid because Dog IS an Animal.
pet = new Cat(); reassigns the SAME variable to a different type.

Animal pet = new Dog(); pet.speak();
pet = new Cat(); pet.speak();

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Polymorphic Array
practice_exercises.description: Store different types in one array, process in a loop.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The loop does not know what each object IS -- polymorphism handles it.

hints.message: Shape[] shapes = { new Circle(5), new Rect(4, 6) };
for (Shape s : shapes) System.out.printf("%s: %.2f%n", s.name, s.area());
Java calls the RIGHT area() for each shape automatically. That is polymorphism.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Polymorphic Method Parameter
practice_exercises.description: Write makeSpeak(Animal a) that works with ANY animal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Method accepts parent type but works with all children.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Asking Rex to speak:
Rex: Woof!
Asking Luna to speak:
Luna: Meow!

hints.message: static void makeSpeak(Animal a) { System.out.println("Asking "+a.name+" to speak:"); a.speak(); }
The method works with Dog, Cat, and ANY future Animal subclass!
static void makeSpeak(Animal a) { System.out.println("Asking "+a.name+" to speak:"); a.speak(); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Predict -- Polymorphism Band
practice_exercises.description: Which play() and tune() runs for each musician?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Override = child version. No override = parent version.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Strumming guitar
Tuning
Beating drums
Tightening heads

hints.message: Guitarist overrides play() only. Drummer overrides both.
Guitarist: play=Strumming, tune=Tuning (parent). Drummer: play=Beating, tune=Tightening.
Output: Strumming guitar, Tuning, Beating drums, Tightening heads

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Factory Method
practice_exercises.description: createNotification returns different types based on input.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Return type is parent, but you return child objects. Factory pattern!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Email: Meeting at 3
SMS: Running late

hints.message: static Notification create(String type, String msg) { if(type.equals("email")) return new Email(msg); ... }
Return type Notification, actual objects are Email or SMS.
static Notification create(String type,String msg) { if("email".equals(type)) return new Email(msg); if("sms".equals(type)) return new SMS(msg); return new Notification(msg); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: instanceof Counting
practice_exercises.description: Count types in a polymorphic array.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: instanceof tells you the ACTUAL type of each object.

hints.message: int cars=0; for (Vehicle v : lot) if (v instanceof Car) cars++;
Use else if for Truck and Moto.
int c=0,t=0,m=0; for(Vehicle v:lot){if(v instanceof Car)c++;else if(v instanceof Truck)t++;else if(v instanceof Moto)m++;} System.out.println("Cars:"+c+", Trucks:"+t+", Motos:"+m);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Safe Downcasting
practice_exercises.description: Check instanceof then cast to call child-specific methods.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: ALWAYS check instanceof before casting. No check = crash risk.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Rex fetches!
Luna climbs!
Nemo swims!

hints.message: if (p instanceof Dog) { Dog d = (Dog) p; d.fetch(); }
Use else if for Cat and Fish with their specific methods.
for(Animal p:pets){if(p instanceof Dog)((Dog)p).fetch();else if(p instanceof Cat)((Cat)p).climb();else if(p instanceof Fish)((Fish)p).swim();}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Polymorphic Total
practice_exercises.description: Calculate total using different finalPrice() overrides.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The loop does not care about types -- polymorphism does the math.

hints.message: static double total(Item[] items) { double t=0; for(Item i:items) t+=i.finalPrice(); return t; }
static double total(Item[] items){double t=0;for(Item i:items)t+=i.finalPrice();return t;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Fix ClassCastException
practice_exercises.description: This code crashes. Add instanceof check.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Casting Cat to Dog = ClassCastException at runtime.

hints.message: The object is a Cat, not a Dog!
Wrap in: if (a instanceof Dog) { Dog d=(Dog)a; d.fetch(); }
if(a instanceof Dog){Dog d=(Dog)a;d.fetch();}else{System.out.println("Not a Dog");}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Tricky Predict -- Internal Call
practice_exercises.description: Parent method calls overridden method.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Even inside parent code, overridden methods use the child version.

hints.message: method2() is in Base (not overridden). But method1() IS overridden.
Even though method2 is in Base, when it calls method1, the ACTUAL object is Derived.
Base.m2 then Derived.m1. This is dynamic dispatch in action.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Payment System
practice_exercises.description: Different payment methods have different fees.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Only override getFee(). getTotal() and receipt() use it automatically.

hints.message: class CreditCard extends Payment { CreditCard(double a){super(a);} @Override double getFee(){return amount*0.03;} }
receipt() calls getFee() which polymorphism routes to the right version.

class CreditCard extends Payment{CreditCard(double a){super(a);}double getFee(){return amount*0.03;}}
class BankTransfer extends Payment{BankTransfer(double a){super(a);}double getFee(){return 1.50;}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Zoo Simulator
practice_exercises.description: Build abstract Animal with Lion, Parrot, Snake.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine abstract class + polymorphic array + overrides.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Simba the Lion
Simba ROARS!

Polly the Parrot
Polly says: Hello!

Kaa the Snake
Kaa hisses: sssss

hints.message: abstract class Animal { String name,species; Animal(String n,String s){name=n;species=s;} abstract void makeSound(); void describe(){System.out.println(name+" the "+species);} }
class Lion extends Animal { Lion(String n){super(n,"Lion");} void makeSound(){System.out.println(name+" ROARS!");} }

class Parrot extends Animal { String phrase; Parrot(String n,String p){super(n,"Parrot");phrase=p;} void makeSound(){System.out.println(name+" says: "+phrase);} }
class Snake extends Animal { Snake(String n){super(n,"Snake");} void makeSound(){System.out.println(name+" hisses: sssss");} }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: First Abstract Class
practice_exercises.description: Create abstract Shape with abstract area().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: abstract = intentionally incomplete. Children fill the blanks.

hints.message: abstract class Shape { abstract double area(); void display(){System.out.printf("Area: %.2f%n",area());} }
class Circle extends Shape { double r; Circle(double r){this.r=r;} @Override double area(){return Math.PI*r*r;} }
Shape cannot be instantiated. display() calls area() which Circle provides.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Abstract with Constructor
practice_exercises.description: Abstract Vehicle with brand, abstract fuelEfficiency().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Abstract classes CAN have constructors!

hints.message: abstract class Vehicle{String b;Vehicle(String b){this.b=b;}abstract double fuelEff();void tripCost(int km,double p){System.out.printf("%s: %dkm costs $%.2f%n",b,km,(km/fuelEff())*p);}}
class Car extends Vehicle{double kpl;Car(String b,double k){super(b);kpl=k;}double fuelEff(){return kpl;}}
class ElectricCar extends Vehicle{ElectricCar(String b){super(b);}double fuelEff(){return 999;}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Fix -- Cannot Instantiate Abstract
practice_exercises.description: Code tries to create abstract object. Fix it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create a concrete subclass instead!

hints.message: Create class Water extends Drink { ... }
Water() { super("Water"); } @Override void pour() { System.out.println("Pouring "+name); }

class Water extends Drink{Water(){super("Water");}void pour(){System.out.println("Pouring "+name);}}
In main: Drink d = new Water(); d.pour();

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Abstract Animal Kingdom
practice_exercises.description: Abstract Animal with sound() and legs(). Dog, Spider, Snake.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each animal has different sounds and leg counts.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Rex has 4 legs and says Woof
Charlotte has 8 legs and says ...
Kaa has 0 legs and says Hiss

hints.message: abstract class Animal{String name;Animal(String n){name=n;}abstract String sound();abstract int legs();void info(){System.out.println(name+" has "+legs()+" legs and says "+sound());}}
class Dog extends Animal{Dog(String n){super(n);}String sound(){return"Woof";}int legs(){return 4;}}

class Spider extends Animal{Spider(String n){super(n);}String sound(){return"...";}int legs(){return 8;}}
class Snake extends Animal{Snake(String n){super(n);}String sound(){return"Hiss";}int legs(){return 0;}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Template Method Pattern
practice_exercises.description: Parent defines steps, children fill in details.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The process() method locks the order. Children customise each step.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Reading CSV...
Parsing rows...
Writing to DB...
Done!
Reading JSON...
Parsing objects...
Writing to API...
Done!

hints.message: class CSVProcessor extends DataProcessor { void readData(){System.out.println("Reading CSV...");} ... }
Implement all 3 abstract methods in each subclass.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Refactor to Abstract
practice_exercises.description: Remove duplicate code by creating abstract Employee.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Both classes share name and payslip(). Only monthlyPay() differs.

hints.message: abstract class Employee{String name;Employee(String n){name=n;}abstract double monthlyPay();void payslip(){System.out.printf("%s: $%.2f/month%n",name,monthlyPay());}}
class FullTime extends Employee{double sal;FullTime(String n,double s){super(n);sal=s;}double monthlyPay(){return sal/12;}}
class PartTime extends Employee{double rate;int hrs;PartTime(String n,double r,int h){super(n);rate=r;hrs=h;}double monthlyPay(){return rate*hrs*4;}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Abstract + final Combined
practice_exercises.description: Header is final (locked), content is abstract (must override).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: final = cannot change. abstract = must provide.

hints.message: class SalesReport extends Report { void content(){System.out.println("Sales: $50,000");} }
class BugReport extends Report { void content(){System.out.println("Bugs: 23");} }
header() is final: all reports have same header. content() is abstract: each fills it differently.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Abstract Game Framework
practice_exercises.description: Game with shared addPoints, abstract play().
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Shared scoring + unique gameplay per game type.

hints.message: class QuizGame extends Game{QuizGame(){super("Quiz");}void play(){addPoints(10);addPoints(10);addPoints(10);}}
addPoints() is concrete (shared). play() is abstract (each game different).

class QuizGame extends Game{QuizGame(){super("Quiz");}void play(){addPoints(10);addPoints(10);addPoints(10);}}
class ArcadeGame extends Game{ArcadeGame(){super("Arcade");}void play(){addPoints(100);addPoints(50);}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Predict -- Abstract Method Call
practice_exercises.description: Concrete method calls abstract method. What prints?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: printFormatted calls format() -- which version runs?

hints.message: printFormatted calls format(). Upper returns toUpperCase. Star wraps in stars.
HELLO and *** hello ***
printFormatted is defined once. format() runs the child version each time.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Abstract Vehicle Fleet
practice_exercises.description: Multi-level: abstract Vehicle -> abstract MotorVehicle -> Car, Truck. Plus Bicycle.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Two levels of abstraction. Bicycle is non-motor.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
BMW | Petrol | 300HP | 250km/h
Volvo | Diesel | 500HP | 180km/h
Trek | Human power

hints.message: abstract class Vehicle{String b;Vehicle(String b){this.b=b;}abstract String fuelType();void describe(){System.out.print(b+" | "+fuelType());}}

abstract class MotorVehicle extends Vehicle{int hp;MotorVehicle(String b,int hp){super(b);this.hp=hp;}abstract int topSpeed();@Override void describe(){super.describe();System.out.println(" | "+hp+"HP | "+topSpeed()+"km/h");}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: First Interface
practice_exercises.description: Create Greetable interface. Two classes implement it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: An interface is a contract: you MUST provide these methods.

hints.message: interface Greetable { void greet(); }
class FriendlyPerson implements Greetable { public void greet(){System.out.println("Hello! Nice to meet you!");} }
class ShyPerson implements Greetable { public void greet(){System.out.println("...hi.");} }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Multiple Interfaces
practice_exercises.description: Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable, Walkable.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A class can implement MANY interfaces!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Duck flies!
Duck swims!
Duck walks!

hints.message: class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable, Walkable { ... }
Implement all methods: public void fly(){...} public void swim(){...} public void walk(){...}

class Duck implements Flyable,Swimmable,Walkable{public void fly(){System.out.println("Duck flies!");}public void swim(){System.out.println("Duck swims!");}public void walk(){System.out.println("Duck walks!");}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Interface as Variable Type
practice_exercises.description: Use Calculable interface as method parameter.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Interface types enable polymorphism just like class types.

hints.message: static void show(Calculable c) { System.out.printf("Result: %.2f%n",c.calc()); }
Any class implementing Calculable can be passed!
static void show(Calculable c){System.out.printf("Result: %.2f%n",c.calc());}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: extends + implements
practice_exercises.description: Dog extends Animal AND implements Trainable, Domestic.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A class can extend ONE parent AND implement MANY interfaces.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Rex eats
Rex learned: sit
Owner: Alice

hints.message: class Dog extends Animal implements Trainable, Domestic { String owner; Dog(String n,String o){super(n);owner=o;} ... }
public void learn(String t){System.out.println(name+" learned: "+t);} public String getOwner(){return owner;}

class Dog extends Animal implements Trainable,Domestic{String owner;Dog(String n,String o){super(n);owner=o;}public void learn(String t){System.out.println(name+" learned: "+t);}public String getOwner(){return owner;}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Interface Constants
practice_exercises.description: Variables in interfaces are automatically public static final.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Interface fields are constants. Access: InterfaceName.FIELD.

hints.message: interface GameConfig { int MAX_PLAYERS=4; int MAX_LEVEL=100; String GAME_NAME="Synapse"; }
All fields in interfaces are automatically public static final.
interface GameConfig{int MAX_PLAYERS=4;int MAX_LEVEL=100;String GAME_NAME="Synapse";}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Comparable Interface
practice_exercises.description: Implement Comparable to sort Students by grade.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Comparable is built-in. Override compareTo() for custom sorting.

hints.message: For descending: return other.grade - this.grade;
@Override public int compareTo(Student o) { return o.grade - this.grade; }
compareTo returns negative if THIS should come first. For descending, reverse the subtraction.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Default Methods
practice_exercises.description: Add default printBorder() to Printable interface.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Since Java 8, interfaces can have default methods with code.

hints.message: default void printBorder() { System.out.println("--------------------"); print(); System.out.println("--------------------"); }
default keyword gives the method a body INSIDE the interface.
Message gets printBorder() for free without implementing it!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Interface Segregation
practice_exercises.description: Small focused interfaces instead of one fat one.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Better many small interfaces than one big one.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Alice is coding
Alice is testing
Bob is coding
Bob is managing

hints.message: interface Codeable{void code();} interface Testable{void test();} interface Manageable{void manage();}

class Developer implements Codeable,Testable{String name;Developer(String n){name=n;}public void code(){System.out.println(name+" is coding");}public void test(){System.out.println(name+" is testing");}}

class TeamLead implements Codeable,Manageable{String name;TeamLead(String n){name=n;}public void code(){System.out.println(name+" is coding");}public void manage(){System.out.println(name+" is managing");}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Plugin System
practice_exercises.description: Extensible system using interfaces.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: New plugins plug in without changing existing code.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Run: Spell
Checking spelling...
Run: Grammar
Checking grammar...
Run: Counter
Counting words...

hints.message: class GrammarCheck implements Plugin{public String getName(){return"Grammar";}public void execute(){System.out.println("Checking grammar...");}}
class WordCount implements Plugin{public String getName(){return"Counter";}public void execute(){System.out.println("Counting words...");}}
run() works with ANY Plugin implementation -- even ones not yet written!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Abstract vs Interface -- Choose
practice_exercises.description: Music app: shared fields + methods = abstract class.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Shared state (currentPosition) means abstract class, not interface.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Playing song: Bohemian
Seeking to 60s
Playing podcast: Tech Talk

hints.message: abstract class AudioSource{String title;int pos=0;AudioSource(String t){title=t;}abstract void play();void seekTo(int s){pos=s;System.out.println("Seeking to "+s+"s");}}
class Song extends AudioSource{Song(String t){super(t);}void play(){System.out.println("Playing song: "+title);}}
class Podcast extends AudioSource{Podcast(String t){super(t);}void play(){System.out.println("Playing podcast: "+title);}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Library System
practice_exercises.description: abstract LibraryItem + Borrowable interface.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine abstract class + interface + polymorphism.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
[BOOK] Code (2008)
[DVD] Matrix (1999)
Alice borrowed Code
Code returned

hints.message: abstract class LibraryItem{String title;int year;LibraryItem(String t,int y){title=t;year=y;}abstract String getType();void display(){System.out.println("["+getType()+"] "+title+" ("+year+")");}}
interface Borrowable{void borrow(String p);void returnItem();}

class Book extends LibraryItem implements Borrowable{String borrower;Book(String t,int y){super(t,y);}String getType(){return"BOOK";}public void borrow(String p){borrower=p;System.out.println(p+" borrowed "+title);}public void returnItem(){borrower=null;System.out.println(title+" returned");}}
class DVD extends LibraryItem implements Borrowable{String borrower;DVD(String t,int y){super(t,y);}String getType(){return"DVD";}public void borrow(String p){borrower=p;System.out.println(p+" borrowed "+title);}public void returnItem(){borrower=null;System.out.println(title+" returned");}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Notification Channels
practice_exercises.description: Interface-based notification system.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Different channels, same broadcast method.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
EMAIL >> Maintenance 2AM
SMS >> Maintenance 2AM
PUSH >> Maintenance 2AM

hints.message: class EmailCh implements Channel{public void send(String m){System.out.println("EMAIL >> "+m);}}
Same pattern for SMSCh and PushCh.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Restaurant Menu
practice_exercises.description: Abstract MenuItem with Appetizer, MainCourse, Dessert.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each category returns its own label.

hints.message: class Appetizer extends MenuItem{Appetizer(String n,double p){super(n,p);}String category(){return"STARTER";}}
Same for MainCourse(MAIN) and Dessert(DESSERT).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Shape Drawer with Interface
practice_exercises.description: Drawable interface + abstract Shape = powerful combo.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Some shapes are drawable, some are not.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Circle area: 78.54
Drawing Circle r=5.0
Ghost area: 0.00

hints.message: class Circle extends Shape implements Drawable{double r;Circle(double r){super("Circle");this.r=r;}double area(){return Math.PI*r*r;}public void draw(){System.out.println("Drawing Circle r="+r);}}
class InvisibleShape extends Shape{InvisibleShape(){super("Ghost");}double area(){return 0;}}
Only Circle implements Drawable. instanceof check before calling draw().

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Vehicle Fleet Manager
practice_exercises.description: Count vehicles, calculate total fuel, use polymorphism.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine abstract class, interface, array, instanceof.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
AB-123: 0.08 L/km
EV-001: 0.02 L/km [Battery: 85%]
CD-456: 0.08 L/km
EV-002: 0.02 L/km [Battery: 85%]
EVs: 2

hints.message: Use instanceof Electric to check for EVs.
((Electric)v).batteryLevel() after instanceof check.
This combines abstract Vehicle + Electric interface + polymorphic array + instanceof.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Event Handler System
practice_exercises.description: Interface-based event handling like real GUIs.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Event listeners use interfaces in real Java applications.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Click: Submit clicked
Key: Search clicked

hints.message: class KeyHandler implements EventHandler{public void handle(String e){System.out.println("Key: "+e);}}
class ScrollHandler implements EventHandler{public void handle(String e){System.out.println("Scroll: "+e);}}
Button does not know which handler it uses -- that is the power of interfaces.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Database Connector
practice_exercises.description: Abstract + interface for different DB types.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Real apps use this pattern for database flexibility.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
MySQL connecting to localhost:3306
Connected to: localhost:3306
MySQL result: 5 rows
MySQL disconnected

hints.message: class MySQLConnector extends DBConnector implements Queryable{MySQLConnector(String u){super(u);}void connect(){System.out.println("MySQL connecting to "+url);}void disconnect(){System.out.println("MySQL disconnected");}public String query(String sql){return"MySQL result: 5 rows";}}
Same pattern for SQLiteConnector.
Real apps swap between MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite using this exact pattern.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Shape Calculator App
practice_exercises.description: Full OOP app: abstract Shape, interface Printable, calculate totals.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Everything together in one mini-application.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Circle r=5.0 area=78.54 perim=31.42
Rect 4.0x6.0 area=24.00 perim=20.00
Circle r=3.0 area=28.27 perim=18.85
Total area: 130.81

hints.message: class Circle extends Shape implements Printable{double r;Circle(double r){this.r=r;}double area(){return Math.PI*r*r;}double perimeter(){return 2*Math.PI*r;}public void printInfo(){System.out.printf("Circle r=%.1f area=%.2f perim=%.2f%n",r,area(),perimeter());}}

class Rect extends Shape implements Printable{double w,h;Rect(double w,double h){this.w=w;this.h=h;}double area(){return w*h;}double perimeter(){return 2*(w+h);}public void printInfo(){System.out.printf("Rect %.1fx%.1f area=%.2f perim=%.2f%n",w,h,area(),perimeter());}}
static double totalArea(Shape[] s){double t=0;for(Shape x:s)t+=x.area();return t;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Game Character System
practice_exercises.description: RPG characters with abstract + interface.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Like a real game engine: abstract Character, Attackable, Healable.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Conan attacks Elara for 20 dmg
Arthur heals Elara for 15 HP
Arthur attacks Conan for 15 dmg
Conan(Warrior) HP:85
Elara(Healer) HP:55
Arthur(Paladin) HP:80

hints.message: class Warrior extends GameChar implements Attackable{Warrior(String n,int hp){super(n,hp);}String role(){return"Warrior";}public void attack(GameChar t){t.hp-=20;System.out.println(name+" attacks "+t.name+" for 20 dmg");}}

class Healer extends GameChar implements Healable{Healer(String n,int hp){super(n,hp);}String role(){return"Healer";}public void heal(GameChar t){t.hp+=15;System.out.println(name+" heals "+t.name+" for 15 HP");}}

class Paladin extends GameChar implements Attackable,Healable{Paladin(String n,int hp){super(n,hp);}String role(){return"Paladin";}public void attack(GameChar t){t.hp-=15;System.out.println(name+" attacks "+t.name+" for 15 dmg");}public void heal(GameChar t){t.hp+=15;System.out.println(name+" heals "+t.name+" for 15 HP");}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Full OOP Challenge -- Online Store
practice_exercises.description: Build a complete system: Product hierarchy + Discountable + Cart.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The capstone! Everything you learned in one exercise.

hints.message: abstract class Product{String name;double price;Product(String n,double p){name=n;price=p;}abstract String category();double finalPrice(){return(this instanceof Discountable)?price*(1-((Discountable)this).discount()):price;}}

class Cart{java.util.ArrayList<Product> items=new java.util.ArrayList<>();void addItem(Product p){items.add(p);}double total(){double t=0;for(Product p:items)t+=p.finalPrice();return t;}void printReceipt(){System.out.println("=== RECEIPT ===");for(Product p:items){System.out.printf("[%s] %s $%.2f",p.category(),p.name,p.price);if(p instanceof Discountable)System.out.printf(" -> $%.2f",p.finalPrice());System.out.println();}System.out.printf("Total: $%.2f%n",total());}}
